surface.name: GitHub Hello World web tutorial
surface.selection_basis: Workshop selection policy (2026-07-19): when official docs present peer routes, commit to the fastest and most commonly used documented route. Prefer the vendor's first-listed or recommended quickstart, dashboard/console over CLI when both are peers, and the route with an explicit vendor time claim or fewest documented steps when tied. Committed to already-reconstructed surface `GitHub Hello World web tutorial`. Prior basis: GitHub's Start your journey track presents Hello World immediately after account creation. The tutorial explicitly teaches the GitHub flow in the browser and ends after a repository, branch, committed README change, pull request, merge, and branch deletion. It requires no coding, CLI, Git installation, or developer API token.

alternatives_considered.name: GitHub REST API getting started
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: It is the API-consumer route and adds authentication, headers, API versions, rate limits, and client choices after a GitHub account exists.

alternatives_considered.name: Git command-line workflow
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: It adds local Git installation, configuration, clone, branch, commit, and push; Hello World intentionally teaches the same collaboration concepts in the browser.

alternatives_considered.name: GitHub CLI
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: It adds gh installation and authentication and is not the first journey tutorial.

alternatives_considered.name: GitHub Apps, Actions, Codespaces, and Packages
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: Each is a separate developer platform with its own app, workflow, environment, or package success boundary.

entry_point.developer_intent: Complete GitHub's first project workflow entirely in the browser: create a repository, branch, commit, pull request, merge, and verify the published README change.
assumed_prior_state: Discovery is complete and the developer has selected GitHub's Hello World journey.
The developer may not yet have a GitHub account.
No local Git, GitHub CLI, code editor, or API token is assumed.

documented_first_success.normalized_outcome: The developer creates hello-world with a README, creates readme-edits, commits a README change, opens a pull request, merges it into main, deletes the branch, and sees the published change on the Code tab.
documented_first_success.observable_completion_signal: GitHub reports the pull request was successfully merged and closed, and the hello-world Code tab shows the committed README change on main.

boundary_evidence.official_label_or_terminal_state: Step 5 says the merge confirmation produces a successfully merged and closed message, then directs the learner to Code to see published changes; Conclusion says the tutorial is complete.

documented_first_success.why_this_is_the_boundary: It proves account access and the complete GitHub collaboration loop from repository creation through durable merged change. Profile customization, command-line Git, external collaboration, APIs, and automation follow later.

prerequisites.requirement: A personal GitHub account is required; account creation requires signup and email verification according to the preceding journey guide.
A supported web browser and network access to GitHub are required; the tutorial does not require Git or a coding environment.
The tutorial asks the learner to choose Public or Private repository visibility and does not document a payment-card or paid-plan gate for either tutorial choice.

primary_path.action: Create or sign in to a personal GitHub account and complete required email verification.
primary_path.input: Account identity, username, password or supported sign-in, and email
primary_path.success_signal: The GitHub dashboard and New repository action are accessible.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Username availability, email verification, CAPTCHA, flagged-account, or organization SSO paths can add steps not repeated in Hello World.

primary_path.action: Select New repository, set Repository name to hello-world, type a short description, select Public or Private, select Add a README file, and select Create repository.
details: The guide's example description is This repository is for practicing the GitHub Flow.
Visibility is an explicit learner choice in the current guide, not a mandate to use Public.
primary_path.input: Repository name, short description, visibility choice, and README choice
primary_path.output: hello-world repository of the selected visibility with main branch and README
primary_path.success_signal: The repository Code tab shows README.md on main.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: A name conflict, policy restriction on the selected visibility, or omitted description or README changes the tutorial state and requires correction.

primary_path.action: Open the branch dropdown from main, type readme-edits, and select Create branch: readme-edits from main.
primary_path.success_signal: The repository branch selector shows readme-edits.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Branch-protection, naming policy, permissions, or selecting an existing branch can block creation.

primary_path.action: On readme-edits, open README.md, select Edit, and write a short description about yourself.
primary_path.success_signal: The editor diff shows the intended addition on readme-edits.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Editing main or another file changes the intended tutorial comparison.

primary_path.action: Select Commit changes, enter a descriptive commit message, and select Commit changes again.
details: The commit is made only on readme-edits.
primary_path.success_signal: The branch contains a commit and differs from main.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Missing permissions, policy checks, or abandoning the dialog leaves no committed difference for a pull request.

primary_path.action: Open Pull requests, select New pull request, compare readme-edits against main, and review the diff.
primary_path.success_signal: The compare page shows the README additions and no unintended changes.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Wrong base or compare branch, no difference, or unresolved conflicts prevents the intended pull request.

primary_path.action: Select Create pull request, add a title and brief description, and select Create pull request again.
primary_path.output: Open pull request from readme-edits to main
primary_path.success_signal: The pull request Conversation page is open with the expected diff.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Repository policy, permissions, missing comparison, or branch conflict can prevent creation.

primary_path.action: At the bottom of the pull request, select Merge pull request and then Confirm merge.
primary_path.success_signal: GitHub reports the pull request was successfully merged and closed.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Conflicts, required reviews, checks, branch protection, merge queue, or missing permission block merge until satisfied.

details: The merged change remains on main and a new branch can be created for future work.
primary_path.success_signal: readme-edits is removed without removing the merged commit.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Protected-branch rules or selecting the wrong branch can block deletion; deletion is safe only after merge.

primary_path.action: Return to the hello-world Code tab and verify the README change is published on main.
primary_path.success_signal: The about-yourself text appears in README.md and main is selected.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Viewing an old branch, cached page, or failed merge prevents the expected content and requires checking the closed pull request and main history.

branches.condition: The developer has no GitHub account.
branches.path: Open GitHub signup, enter the required identity and credentials, choose an available username, complete verification and CAPTCHA, verify email, then sign in and return to Hello World.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds account creation and verification before repository work.

branches.path: Create hello-world as a Public repository, then continue with the same README, branch, pull-request, merge, and verification steps.
branches.effect_on_first_success: The tutorial repository is visible to everyone on GitHub.

branches.path: Create hello-world as a Private repository, then continue with the same README, branch, pull-request, merge, and verification steps.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Repository access is restricted, but the tutorial completion signal is unchanged.

branches.condition: The compare page shows unintended changes.
branches.path: Return to readme-edits, correct or revert the unintended file changes, commit the correction, reopen comparison, and create the pull request only when the diff is right.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds another edit and commit before pull-request creation.

branches.path: Resolve the conflicting code in a commit or discuss it in pull-request comments, update the branch, wait for conflict status to clear, then retry merge and confirm.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds conflict resolution and another review before merge.

branches.condition: Branch protection requires reviews or checks.
branches.path: Obtain required reviews and wait for required status checks or merge-queue conditions, then merge using the permitted method.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds collaborators, automation, and waiting that the personal tutorial does not expect.

branches.condition: A local Git or CLI route is preferred.
branches.path: Install and configure Git or GitHub CLI, authenticate, create or clone the repository, create readme-edits locally, edit and commit README, push the branch, open and merge a pull request, and pull or inspect main.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds installation, authentication, local clone, remote, push, and synchronization steps.

branches.condition: The intended surface is GitHub REST API rather than collaboration basics.
branches.path: Choose an API authentication method, create or obtain a token with minimum permissions, set Accept, Authorization, X-GitHub-Api-Version, and User-Agent headers as applicable, send the documented first REST request, and verify its JSON response and rate-limit headers.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Changes the outcome from a merged project change to an authenticated API response and adds token security.

friction_gates.description: GitHub account signup and email verification are required if the learner has no account.
Repository owner, name, required short description, Public or Private visibility choice, and README initialization determine whether the remaining tutorial matches.
The branch must be created from main and selected before editing.
A committed difference between readme-edits and main is required to open a meaningful pull request.
Organization branch protection can add reviews, checks, permissions, merge queue, or conflict-resolution gates even though the personal tutorial expects none.

sources.title: Getting started with the REST API

uncertainties.question: Should GitHub's canonical workshop first mile be collaboration basics or the developer REST API?
uncertainties.impact: The former produces a merged project change with no local tooling; the latter produces an authenticated JSON response and adds token, header, permission, and rate-limit steps.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: GitHub has separate official getting-started guides for each, and the roster label does not choose a surface.

uncertainties.question: Will participants use personal accounts or organization-owned repositories?
uncertainties.impact: Organization ownership can add SSO, repository policies, visibility restrictions, approvals, branch protection, required reviews, checks, and merge queues.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: Hello World intentionally uses a personal repository and cannot establish a participant organization's policies.

uncertainties.question: What signup verification and anti-abuse screens will a new account encounter?
uncertainties.impact: They can add CAPTCHA, email wait, phone or identity review, and flagged-account recovery before repository creation.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: The account guide covers the required outcome but live anti-abuse paths are conditional.

excluded_after_success: Local Git, GitHub Desktop, or GitHub CLI unless selected
GitHub REST or GraphQL API authentication unless selected
Actions, Codespaces, Packages, Pages, Apps, webhooks, Copilot, and security features
Organization SSO and enterprise policy administration
Further commits and pull requests beyond the merged README
